messages.content: A bubble at the clock input of a flip-flop indicates what type of level or edge trigger (clocking)?
A bubble at the clock input of a flip-flop indicates a negative-edge (also known as falling-edge) trigger.
It is the minimum length of time the data bit must be present before the CLK edge hits.

The statement is referring to the setup time of a flip-flop. The setup time is the minimum amount of time the data input signal must be stable before the active edge of the clock signal arrives. If the data input signal changes too close to the clock edge, the flip-flop may not capture the correct value and introduce errors into the circuit.
It is the minimum length of time the data bit must be present after the CLK edge has struck.

The statement is referring to the hold time of a flip-flop. The hold time is the minimum amount of time that the data input signal must remain stable after the active edge of the clock signal has occurred. The purpose of the hold time is to ensure that the data input signal remains stable long enough for the flip-flop to capture the correct value and to avoid introducing errors into the circuit. If the data input signal changes too soon after the clock edge, the flip-flop may not capture the correct value and introduce errors into the circuit.
In a master-slave flip-flop, the slave flip-flop responds to an input during the _______ edge of the clock.

In a master-slave flip-flop, the slave flip-flop responds to an input during the trailing edge (also known as the negative edge or falling edge) of the clock. This is because the output of the master flip-flop is connected to the input of the slave flip-flop, and the slave flip-flop captures the value of the master flip-flop on the trailing edge of the clock signal.
In a master-slave flip-flop, the master flip-flop responds to an input during the _______ edge of the clock.

In a master-slave flip-flop, the master flip-flop responds to an input during the leading edge (also known as the positive edge or rising edge) of the clock. The input data is stored in the master flip-flop on the rising edge of the clock, and then the output of the master flip-flop is fed to the input of the slave flip-flop. The slave flip-flop captures the value of the master flip-flop on the falling edge of the clock. This is the reason the master-slave flip-flop is also known as a "pulse-triggered" flip-flop.
This represents the amount of time it takes for the output of a gate or flip-flop to change states.

The statement is referring to the propagation delay of a gate or flip-flop. Propagation delay is the time it takes for the output of a gate or flip-flop to change states after a change on its input has occurred. It is typically measured from the 50% point of the input signal to the 50% point of the output signal. Propagation delay is an important consideration in digital circuits because it can cause timing issues and affect the overall performance of the circuit.
A priority input that sets to the output Q = 1.

The statement is referring to a "set" input of a flip-flop. When the set input is "high" or active, it will force the output Q to be "high" or a logical "1". The set input takes priority over all other inputs to the flip-flop, hence the term "priority input". In other words, regardless of the state of other inputs, if the set input is activated, it will override them and set the output to a logical "1".
A priority input that sets to the output Q = 0.

The statement is referring to a "reset" input of a flip-flop. When the reset input is "high" or active, it will force the output Q to be "low" or a logical "0". The reset input takes priority over all other inputs to the flip-flop, hence the term "priority input". In other words, regardless of the state of other inputs, if the reset input is activated, it will override them and reset the output to a logical "0".
A type of clocking wherein the flip-flop produces output only on the rising (or falling) edge of the clock signal.

The statement is referring to edge-triggered clocking. In edge-triggered clocking, the flip-flop produces an output only on the rising or falling edge (depending on the type of flip-flop) of the clock signal. This is in contrast to level-triggered clocking, where the flip-flop produces an output whenever the clock signal is in a particular state (high or low). Edge-triggered clocking is commonly used in digital circuits as it can improve the timing behavior and reduce the likelihood of metastable states, where the flip-flop output may oscillate between two values before settling on a stable output.
